For example, regarding front and rear row space, if you pull the front seat all the way back and measure, you'll see the Xforce is noticeably roomier. As for the steering wheel, the Yaris needs to be described as "stiff" to be accurate, whereas the Xforce only gets slightly heavier at high speeds but is otherwise very smooth. The rear seats of the Xforce recline in multiple positions, which is very comfortable for the family. About the armrest, you said the Yaris's is more common—that's true—but saying it's more convenient isn't correct. In many situations, like when my wife sits in the back and needs to grab something, she doesn't need to stop on the highway, and when I park with the tail close to a wall, I don't need to move the car forward to open the trunk and get things out.

But if someone asked me to recommend between these two models, I'd suggest the Yaris Cross. I used to be against the YC myself because of all the controversy around it. But when you really compare them, the quality of these two cars is about the same; it's just that the Xforce has more body lines, giving it a stiffer feel, while the YC's body is smooth and flat. But for 55 million, I think trading off some features for driving feel is acceptable; you can learn to get used to it.

content_original: Mình v?a ng?i th? 3 con XForce Ultimate, Xpander Premium và c? Yaris Cross b?n x?ng  hôm 9/10/2025 thì có nh?n xét sau:
content_english:
I just test drove 3 cars — the XForce Ultimate, Xpander Premium, and the gas version of the Yaris Cross — on 9/10/2025, and here are my observations:

- XForce: Smooth ride, light throttle, quick acceleration, quieter than the other two. Second-row space is roomier than the YC, but I still didn't feel comfortable because the distance from the head to the ceiling is quite close, giving a slightly stuffy feeling. Legroom when stretched forward is fine, but if you tuck your heels in and they hit the seat, it's a bit annoying. I noticed this same issue when I sat in the YC, Corolla Cross, and Camry HEV at the showroom — it's pretty similar. Probably a common design thing. I prefer the YC's interior though.

- Xpander Premium: Looks good, spacious. The driver/passenger seats and second row are all comfortable to sit in, with no discomfort in the legs or head. Haven't tried the third row, so I can't comment. Ride isn't as smooth as the XForce, throttle feels heavier, and acceleration feels slower than the XForce.

- YC gas: Interior is better than the XForce in my opinion. Throttle response isn't jerky and it's quieter than the XForce. In the second row, your legs feel a bit uncomfortable if you spread them out to the sides. If you tuck both legs under the front seat or cross your legs, it's fine. Oh, I'm 1m68 tall. But the YC's ceiling doesn't bother me like the XForce's. Overall, the YC wins with full ADAS, power-adjustable seats, and a 360° camera, beating the XForce. In terms of wheel size, one is 9.5 inches and the other is 10. But the XForce's second-row armrest can be lifted to access the rear cup holders, which is convenient.

- On-road prices at the time I'm posting this comment for SG plates, family cars: XForce Premium: 685, XForce Ultimate: 722, Xpander Premium: 670, and YC gas: 700.

Every car has its pros and cons. Oh, I only drove around 4-5km in the city, haven't had a chance to go further to really experience them. Still can't decide which one to buy.

content_original: Mình v?a ng?i th? 3 con XForce Ultimate, Xpander Premium và c? Yaris Cross b?n x?ng  hôm 9/10/2025 thì có nh?n xét sau:
category: Styling / Design; Powertrain / Performance; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Safety & ADAS; Safety & ADAS; New Tech
parameter: Interior Design; Acceleration; Head Room; Leg Room; ADAS Features; 360-degree Camera; Electric Seats
content_english:
I just test drove 3 cars: XForce Ultimate, Xpander Premium, and the gasoline Yaris Cross on 9/10/2025 and have the following observations:

- XForce: The car runs smoothly, light throttle, quick acceleration, quieter than the other two. Second-row seating space is wider than the YC, but I still didn't feel comfortable because the distance from head to ceiling is quite close, causing a slightly stuffy feeling. Legs stretch forward comfortably, but if you pull your heels inward, they touch the seat, which I found a bit annoying. I also noticed this in the YC, Corolla Cross, and Camry HEV at the showroom – it's pretty much the same. Probably due to the common design. As for the interior, I prefer the YC's.

- Xpander Premium: Beautiful, spacious. The driver/passenger seats and second row are comfortable to sit in, no discomfort in the legs or head. I haven't tried the third row yet, so I don't know. Ride isn't as smooth as the XForce, throttle feels stiffer, acceleration feels slower than the XForce.

- Gasoline YC: Interior I find OK compared to the XForce. Throttle response doesn't have that abrupt jolt, but it's noisier than the XForce. In the second row, legs are a bit uncomfortable if spread out to the sides. If you tuck your legs under the front seat or sit cross-legged, it's fine. Oh, I'm 1m68 tall. But the YC's ceiling doesn't make me uncomfortable like the XForce's. Overall, the YC has full ADAS, power seats, and 360-degree camera, which beats the XForce. As for the spare tire, both the YC and XForce have one – one is 9.5 inches, the other is 10 inches. But the XForce's second-row armrest can be pulled to access the spare tire at the back, which is convenient.

- On-road price at the time I post this comment for SG license plates, family cars as follows: XForce Premium: 685, XForce Ultimate: 722, XPander Premium: 670, and YC gasoline: 700.

Each car has its own strengths and weaknesses. Oh, I only drove around 4–5 km in the city, haven't had a chance to go further to experience. Still can't decide which car to buy yet.

In the A and B segments, when it comes to crash safety, every car is equally flimsy—there’s no Daihatsu that’s any sturdier than other A and B segment cars from other brands. Drive safely and try to be careful; as for safety features, Toyota has now pretty much equipped them across all models. In terms of design, I still think Daihatsu’s designs look better than Hyundai’s—for instance, the Veloz still looks better than the Stargazer, the Yaris Cross still looks better than the Creta, not to mention the air conditioning is much cooler than many Korean cars. As for sound insulation, people complain they’re tin cans, but I still find them quieter than Honda. As for the engine, the horsepower is similar to Mitsubishi, but it feels more responsive and stronger than Mitsubishi by a fair bit. Anyway, to each their own—buy what you like, this is just for fun discussion. If anyone thinks I’m wrong, reply and let me re-evaluate.

parameter: Air Conditioning; Steering Feel; Ride Quality; Energy Consumption; NVH; NVH; Interior Design; Brand Image; Maintenance Cost
content_english: I've driven the Vios, Sorento, Xforce, new Seltos, and Yaris Cross, so it's not like I don't know the pros and cons of those cars, but after weighing all the criteria against my needs, I chose this one. The air conditioning is deep and cold—at 26°C it's already very cool. The steering isn't light, but the wheel feels very solid and grip is tight, not loose and wobbly like some rambling reviewers claim. The suspension is really satisfying on city roads, and I work at the border—roads aren't good at all. Fuel economy is economical, around 5-6L/100km depending on how heavy or light your foot is. Cabin sound insulation isn't as bad as those reviewers say; actually it's pretty good. The engine bay insulation isn't very good, but the initial throttle response is smooth. If you want a nicer interior, just add more money. Its sales are on top in Thailand, and in July it was over 1,200 units in Vietnam—not struggling like some brands with crazy pricing. Maintenance can sometimes be only half of what certain brands charge.

content_english:
The Mercedes in Germany that had a pedal misapplication, causing the car to lunge forward and slam into a row of motorcycles, resulting in one death and several serious injuries—this happened because the car did not have an "active safety system."

If it had, this system would have activated emergency braking, and the car would not have caused such a severe accident.

To all those who earn a living and do good deeds, when buying a car, please choose one with an "active safety system." This is something you can't see like the interior, but it is extremely valuable.

Driving skills and vehicle control are important, but safety technology is there to assist the driver in emergencies like the pedal misapplication accident above.

Don't be annoyed when it suddenly brakes and startles you; better to let it bother you for a lifetime of driving than to regret it for a lifetime like that woman driving the Mercedes.

content_english: I've driven the Vios, Sorento, Xforce, new Seltos, and Yaris Cross already, so it's not like I don't know the pros and cons of those cars. But when weighing every criterion against my needs, I chose this one. The air conditioning is cool and deep — set to 26°C and it's already very cold. The steering isn't the sharpest, but the wheel feels very solid and holds its line firmly, not loose and wobbly like some rambling reviewers claim. The suspension on rough roads is very satisfying — and I'm commuting to the border area, where the roads aren't good at all. Fuel economy is around 5-6 liters per 100 km depending on whether you have a heavy or light foot. Cabin noise insulation? It's not bad like some reviewers say — actually it's pretty good, to be accurate. The only thing is the engine bay isn't that well insulated, but at initial throttle it's smooth. If you want a nicer interior, just spend more money. And its sales are on top in Thailand, right? In July it sold over 1,200 units in Vietnam — it's not flopping like some overpriced brands out there. As for maintenance, it can be as low as half of what some other brands charge.

category: Styling / Design; Styling / Design; New Tech; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Styling / Design; Value Perception
parameter: Exterior Design; Interior Design; Technology Features; Energy Consumption; Driving Smoothness; Steering Precision; Interior Material Quality; Value for Money
content_english:
I'm currently using this car, Yaris Cross 2023 hybrid. The on-road price in Pleiku and Kontum is 838 million VND (with many promotions), plus some accessories, it's 860 million VND on the road. My honest personal assessment:  
1. Exterior: 7/10  
2. Interior: 7/10  
3. Technology: 8/10  
4. Performance: 9/10  

Regarding performance, it's smooth on electric and very powerful when using gasoline and electric combined. And why 9/10? Because it only uses 3.8 liters of gasoline per 100 km, which is barely more than the SH scooter you guys are riding.  

Disadvantages for me: the steering feels a bit strange, meaning it's not as precise down to the smallest detail as my Colorado pickup truck, so I don't feel comfortable. Maybe because my car has more grip on the steering wheel, especially the firmness, so when I turn just a little, it already responds, very responsive and accurate.  

Second disadvantage: the seat cover on the rear seat behind the driver and passenger is not uniform in appearance. It should have been one type, but here there are two types, two styles, which makes it look a bit cheap when you see and touch the back of the driver's seat.  

Those are the only two things I'm not satisfied with about this car; everything else is excellent. If you have the chance, go experience it and judge for yourself! At a price of nearly 900 million VND for a B-segment SUV, I think it's unbeatable.

content_english: I’m currently driving the XF EX trim, and I see your review is spot on about the car—light steering on the highway, and the build quality isn’t quite up to par. The 1.5-liter engine with 104 horsepower on the highway is just average, not weak but nothing impressive. I recently took a trip from Hai Phong to Hoi An, nearly 2,000 km round trip, and a few days ago I went to Yen Bai from Hai Phong, mostly on mountain passes. In that price range, I feel the car performs fairly well—extremely fuel efficient, and the Nano X air conditioning keeps the cabin airy so passengers don’t get carsick. That’s something the HRV probably struggles with. I’m 1.78m tall, and when I sit in the back of an HRV, my knees touch the front seat and my head hits the roof. As for performance, I’d lean more toward the HRV because it feels quicker and drives more solidly. But within the price range of over 600 million VND, the EX trim is worth it for what it offers. Thanks for the review—sorry for the long comment.

category: New Tech; Styling / Design; Other; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; Styling / Design; New Tech; Safety & ADAS; Comfort & Space; New Tech; Powertrain / Performance; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Value Perception
parameter: Reverse Camera Quality; Interior Color Options; Brake Noise; Exterior Design; Cabin Space; Interior Material Quality; Infotainment Display; ADAS Features; NVH; Audio System Quality; Driving Dynamics; Visibility; Energy Consumption; Competitor Comparison
content_english:
I'm using an Xforce Ultimate. Took delivery on 20/11/24. After using it for a while, here are the pros and cons:

1. Cons:
- The reverse camera isn't sharp.
- Interior only comes in black. Looks a bit dark. Gets dirty quickly.
- After about 5,000km, there's a fairly loud noise when lightly braking at low speeds. Already contacted the dealer. They said they'll replace the brake discs. I'm waiting for the parts to arrive. Haven't replaced them yet, but this time the noise stopped when driving again.

2. Pros:
- Looks great and spacious. Very imposing exterior. Interior materials are okay. Large screen.
- More than enough safety features: electronic parking brake, auto hold, automatic headlights, automatic wipers, collision mitigation warning, tire pressure sensors, etc. Generally comfortable to use.
- Good sound insulation. Speakers sound really nice.
- Very smooth to drive. Good visibility.
- Very fuel efficient. Highway driving gets under 5L/100km.

I haven't driven the HR-V. But I'd probably still choose the Xforce because it's more spacious and airy.

category: Brand Perception; Styling / Design; Value Perception; Value Perception; New Tech
parameter: Brand Trust; Design Freshness; Fuel Efficiency; Maintenance Cost; Feature Content
content_english:
I see many people obsessed with the CX-5, but the main issue is what if they simply don't like the CX-5's design? They only want something new and current. When spending over 700-800 million, can you even tell the model apart? For example, the CX-5 2017 → 2023, is there really much of a difference? Last month, thanks to the price cut, the CX-5 made a splash and sold a few dozen units faster than the Corolla Cross, but the Toyota brand has a huge influence on Vietnamese buyers. That logo is deeply ingrained. Toyota stays the same, while Mazda drops prices to the bone. (Oh, and the facelift design of the CX-5 cost them practically nothing, right?). For me, in 2023, when spending 700-800 million on a car:

First, you have to pick a big brand — for your friends.
Second, a new design and instant recognition — "Oh, you just bought a Yaris Cross!"
Third, full options.
Next, fuel economy.
Finally, maintenance must be dirt cheap.

I see many people are obsessed with the CX-5, right? The main issue is, what if people don't like the CX-5's design? They only want something new and current. When you spend over 700-800 million and there's no model differentiation? For example, the CX-5 from 2017 to 2023 is hardly different at all. Last month, thanks to the price cut, the CX-5 caused a storm, selling a few dozen units faster than the Corolla Cross, but the Toyota brand has a huge influence on Vietnamese people. This logo is deeply ingrained. Toyota stays the same while Mazda cuts prices to the bone. (Oh, and the generation design of the CX-5 costs them nothing, so why don't they change it?)

For me now in 2023, when spending 700-800 million on a car:

First: You have to choose a big brand. Durable.
Second: New design and recognition—so you can tell at a glance, "Oh, you just bought a Yaris Cross!"
Third: Full options.
Next: Fuel efficiency.
Finally: Maintenance must be cheap, cheap, cheap.

That's what I like. Otherwise, buying a CX-5 just means you're going back to some year? What generation is that?

content_original: Theo dõi kênh ?ã lâu, nay m?i dám comment ?. Em là ng??i dùng Xforce Ultimate, l?y xe tháng 01/2025, xe em là lô s?n xu?
content_english:
I've been following the channel for a long time, but only now dare to comment. I am an Xforce Ultimate user, got the car in January 2025, my car is from the December 2024 production batch, so probably the issues have been fixed already, because my car doesn't have the rattling noise and other common Xforce problems.

When choosing a car, I first eliminated the Honda HRV-L (because its price is over 800 million VND, and also because of the cramped rear seats; I often go back to the countryside carrying about 3 adults in the back, and they really can't sit comfortably). Next, I also didn't choose the Creta and Seltos (because on the Tipcar channel, there was a comparison video saying that maintenance from 0 to 100,000 km for these two cars costs over 50 million VND, while Japanese cars only cost around 20 million). Honestly, I don't like the design of the Creta, and the Seltos with ADAS is too expensive. (I'm a new driver who often drives on highways, so I really like having ADAS.)

So I was left with only two choices: the Yaris Cross and the Xforce Ultimate. When I went to the dealership to see the cars, I liked the Xforce more because it looks more masculine and a bit more spacious. Afterwards, I went home and watched the review videos of both cars and the comparison video on the channel many times. Finally, I decided to buy the Xforce Ultimate.

Just last weekend, I carried 4 adults, 2 children (one in 7th grade and one in 9th grade), plus one more 4-year-old sitting in the front row, and everyone said they were very comfortable, spacious, and didn't get carsick.

As for fuel consumption, when driving in the city, the computer shows under 6L/100km; on a mixed route from Thai Binh to Son Tay, it showed around 4.5L/100km (that's with traffic on Belt Road 3; if not, it would only show a little over 4L/100km). In reality, it might be a bit higher, but I find it very economical. Sometimes my wife even says I drive forever without filling up.

Regarding maintenance, I just had my first service at the dealership at around 2,000 km, and it cost about 1.1 million VND, which is not too expensive. (I see in the Xforce group that many people have had larger services that are also very cheap, and this was also a criterion for me to buy this car.)

In summary, if you need driving feel and only use the first two rows, you should choose the HRV. But for someone like me, a new driver who doesn't know what driving feel is and prioritizes family, you should choose the Xforce.

In the video, I saw that Mr. Viet Anh did not mention that the Xforce has driving modes. The other day when I was driving in the rain and used the WET mode, I felt the steering wheel become very heavy. And if you want to overtake and have a sporty feel, pressing the DS button on the steering wheel gives very quick acceleration. Several times on the highway, I pressed the DS button and gave a little gas, and it reached nearly 100 km/h immediately.

content_english:
I've been following the channel for a long time, but only today I dared to comment. I am a user of the Xforce Ultimate, got the car in January 2025, and my car is from the December 2024 production batch, so maybe all the issues have been fixed already because my car doesn't have the rattling noise or other common Xforce problems.

When choosing a car, I first ruled out the Honda HRV - L (because it's too expensive, over 800 million VND, and the rear seat is cramped—since I often go back to my hometown with about 3 adults sitting in the back, it's really not comfortable). Next, I also didn't choose the Creta and Seltos (because on the Tipcar channel there's a video comparing cars and it says that maintenance from 0 to 100,000 km for these two costs over 50 million VND, while Japanese cars only cost around 20 million VND).

Honestly, I don't like the Creta's design, and the high-end Seltos with ADAS is too expensive. (I'm a new driver who often drives on the highway, so I really wanted ADAS.)

So I only had two options left: the Yaris Cross and the Xforce Ultimate. When I went to the dealership to see the cars, I liked the Xforce more because it looks more masculine and is slightly roomier. After that, I went back and watched the review videos for both cars many times, as well as the comparison video on the channel. In the end, I decided to buy the Xforce Ultimate.

Last weekend, I had 4 adults and 2 kids (one in 7th grade and one in 9th grade) plus another 4-year-old sitting in the front row, and everyone said it was very comfortable and spacious, and no one got carsick.

As for fuel consumption, when driving in the city, the car shows under 6L/100km; when driving mixed from Thai Binh to Son Tay, it shows around 4.5L/100km (that's with traffic on the third ring road; without that, it shows just a bit over 4L/100km). In reality, it might be a bit higher, but I find it very economical. Sometimes I come home and my wife asks why I haven't refueled yet.

Regarding maintenance, I just had my first service at the dealership at nearly 2,000 km, and it cost about 1.1 million VND, which isn't too expensive. (I see in the Xforce group that many people's larger maintenance intervals are also very affordable, and this was one of my criteria for buying this car.)

In summary, I think if you care about driving feel and only use the first two rows, you should choose the HRV. But if you're like me—a new driver who doesn't know what driving feel is and prioritizes family—then you should choose the Xforce.

In the video, I noticed that Viet Anh didn't mention the Xforce's driving modes. The other day when I was driving in the rain and used the WET mode, I felt the steering wheel get very heavy. And if you want to overtake and have a sporty feel, pressing the DS button on the steering wheel gives you very quick acceleration. Several times on the highway, I pressed the DS button and gave just a little gas, and it reached nearly 100 km/h immediately.

category: Styling / Design; Safety & ADAS; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Brand Perception
parameter: Exterior Design; Airbag System; Interior Design; Rear Seat Headroom; Front Visibility; Brand Trust
content_english: I've been driving the Xforce Pre since April 2024, and my brother drives the CX8 Pre. When we go out with a group, we'll swap cars as needed. I did a lot of research across many models before deciding on the Xforce over the HRV, Yaris Cross, Creta, and CX5 Deluxe in terms of price, costs, long-term usability, and family suitability. When the Xforce concept was launched in Vietnam and also during test drives in Indonesia, I followed it very closely. When the car went on sale, I test-drove both the Xforce Pre and the HRV. Truly, the choice has to fit yourself and your family—pitting them head-to-head makes it hard to decide. I looked at and test-drove the HRV G. Basically, it looks good and fairly sporty, but I didn't choose it because of several factors: lack of airbags, interior feels too cheap, when I sat in the back my head touched the ceiling, and up front the windshield is narrow limiting visibility. Not to mention I often go on road trips with my family, so I was also concerned about Honda's after-sales service. As for the Xforce, it also has some downsides: quite a bit of plastic, fit and finish could be better, a bit noisy, and the ride is a bit stiff. However, I chose it for my own and my family's use because it has many advantages: spacious for both rows and the trunk, proven engine reliability (as shown with the Xpander), fairly modern and user-friendly interior, and fuel-efficient. This past Tet holiday, I went on a 9-day road trip from North to South about 3000 km (Hanoi - Da Nang - Nha Trang - Da Lat - Ho Chi Minh City - Ca Mau and all of the Mekong Delta) and I was extremely satisfied. I upgraded to the Xforce from a sedan, so I needed a car with high ground clearance, durability, fuel economy, good looks, reasonable maintenance, and long-term usability. Wishing Tipcar good health, and may everyone choose the car that best fits their needs, family, and work.

content_english:
I've been following the channel for a long time, but only now have I dared to comment. I'm a user of the Xforce Ultimate, took delivery in January 2025, my car is from the December 2024 production batch, so I guess the issues have all been fixed already because my car doesn't have the rattle or other common Xforce problems.  

When choosing a car, I first ruled out the Honda HRV-L (because the price is way over 800 million, and the back seat is cramped—since I often go back to the countryside with about 3 adults in the back, it's really not comfortable). Next, I also didn't choose the Creta and Seltos (because on the Tipcar channel there's a comparison video that said maintenance from 0 to 100,000 km costs over 50 million for those two, while Japanese cars only cost around 20 million).  

Honestly, I don't like the design of the Creta, and the higher-spec Seltos with ADAS is way too expensive. (I'm a new driver who often drives on the highway, so I really wanted ADAS.)  

So I was left with two choices: the Yaris Cross and the Xforce Ultimate. When I went to the dealership to check them out, I liked the Xforce more because it looks more masculine and is slightly more spacious. Then I went home and watched many review videos of both cars, plus the comparison video on the channel, and in the end I decided to buy the Xforce Ultimate.  

Just last weekend, I drove with 4 adults, 2 older kids (one in 7th grade, one in 9th grade), plus a 4-year-old in the back row, and everyone said it was very comfortable and roomy, and no one got carsick.  

As for fuel consumption, in the city the display shows under 6L/100km; mixed driving from Thai Binh to Son Tay the display showed around 4.5L/100km (that's with some highway on the 3rd ring road; otherwise it's just over 4L/100km). In reality it might be a bit higher, but I find it very economical—sometimes my wife jokes that I drive forever without needing to refuel.  

As for maintenance, I just had my first service at the dealership at nearly 2,000 km for about 1.1 million VND, which isn't too expensive. (I see in the Xforce group that many people report very low maintenance costs at higher intervals, and that was also one of my reasons for buying this car.)  

In short, if you care about driving feel and only use the first two rows, go for the HRV; if you're a new driver like me who doesn't know what driving feel is and prioritizes family comfort, then choose the Xforce.  

In the video, I noticed that Viet Anh didn't mention the Xforce's driving modes. The other day I drove in the rain and used WET mode, and the steering wheel felt very heavy. If you want to overtake and have a sportier feel, pressing the DS button on the steering wheel gives very quick acceleration—several times on the highway I pressed DS and gave a little gas, and it reached nearly 100 km/h immediately.

I'm a Xforce Ultimate user, took delivery in January 2025, and my car is from the December 2024 production batch, so I guess it's already had all the issues fixed because my car doesn't have the rattling noise or other common Xforce problems.

When I was choosing a car, I first ruled out the Honda HR-V L (because the price is too high at over 800 million VND, plus the rear seat is cramped—since I often go back to my hometown with about 3 adults in the back, it's really not comfortable). Next, I also didn't go for the Creta and Seltos (because on the Tipcar channel there's a video comparing cars that said maintenance from 0-100,000 km for these two costs over 50 million VND, while Japanese cars only cost around 20 million VND).

Honestly, I don't like the Creta's design, and the high-spec Seltos with ADAS is way too expensive (I'm a new driver who often drives on highways, so I really wanted ADAS).

So I was left with two choices: the Yaris Cross and the Xforce Ultimate. When I went to the dealership to see them, I liked the Xforce more because it looks more masculine and is slightly more spacious. After that, I watched and rewatched many review videos of both cars, including comparison videos on the channel, and in the end, I decided to buy the Xforce Ultimate.

Just last weekend, I had 4 adults and 2 kids (one in 7th grade and one in 9th grade) plus a 4-year-old in the front row, and everyone said they were very comfortable and spacious, and no one got carsick.

As for fuel consumption, driving in the city the display shows under 6L/100km; for mixed driving from Thai Binh to Son Tay, it shows about 4.5L/100km (that includes some traffic on the Belt Road 3; otherwise, it would show just over 4L/100km). In reality, it might be a bit higher, but I find it very economical—sometimes my wife jokes that I drive forever without needing to refuel.

Regarding maintenance, I just had my first service at the dealership at nearly 2,000 km, which cost about 1.1 million VND—not too expensive (I see in the Xforce group that many people have had service at higher intervals for very low costs, and that was also a key factor for me choosing this car).

In short, I think if you value driving feel and only use the first two rows, go for the HR-V; but if you're like me—a new driver who doesn't know what driving feel is and prioritizes family—then choose the Xforce.

In the video, I noticed that Viet Anh didn't mention the Xforce's driving modes. The other day I drove in the rain and used WET mode, and the steering wheel felt very heavy. But if you want to overtake and have some driving feel, pressing the DS button on the steering wheel gives very quick acceleration—several times on the highway I pressed DS and tapped the gas, and it went up to nearly 100 km/h instantly.

category: Safety & ADAS; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; Styling / Design; Brand Perception; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Styling / Design; Powertrain / Performance; Value Perception
parameter: Airbag; Interior Design; Headroom; Windshield Design; Brand Trust; Build Quality; Cabin Space; Engine Reliability; Interior Design; Energy Consumption; Value for Money
content_english: I am currently driving an Xforce Pre since April 2024, and my brother drives a CX8 Pre; when we have a full load of people, we will swap cars to suit the situation. I researched quite a number of cars before finalizing the Xforce, weighing it against the HRV, Yaris Cross, Creta, and CX5 Deluxe in terms of price, costs, long-term usability, and family suitability. When the Xforce concept was launched in Vietnam and during the test drives in Indonesia, I followed it very closely. When the commercial version became available, I test drove both the Xforce Pre and the HRV. Truly, the choice has to fit you and your family; it’s really hard to choose just by spec-sheet comparisons. I looked at and test drove the HRV G. Basically, it looks nice and quite sporty, but I didn’t choose it for several reasons: lack of airbags, interior too basic, when I sat in the driver's seat and moved to the rear my head hit the ceiling, and up front the windshield is narrow so visibility is limited. Not to mention I often go on road trips with my family, so I was also worried about Honda’s road noise. The Xforce also has some downsides: a bit too much plastic, fit and finish is slightly poor, some creaks and rattles, and the suspension is a bit stiff. However, I chose it because for my own use and my family, it has quite a few advantages: spacious for both rows and the trunk, proven engine reliability (as demonstrated in the Xpander), modern and user-friendly interior, and fuel efficiency. This past Tet holiday, I went on a north-south road trip for 9 days covering about 3000 km (Hanoi - Da Nang - Nha Trang - Da Lat - Ho Chi Minh City - Ca Mau and all of the Mekong Delta) and I was extremely satisfied. I upgraded to the Xforce from a sedan, so I needed a car with high ground clearance, durability, fuel efficiency, good looks, reasonable maintenance, and long-term usability. Wishing Tipcar good health, and may everyone find the car that best suits their needs, family, and work.

category: Powertrain / Performance; Value Perception; Safety & ADAS
parameter: Engine Power; Feature Content; Safety Features
content_english: Vietnamese people always have the mindset that whatever is stronger is better and more enjoyable. (But they forget the most important and practical factor: Vietnam’s traffic system and speed regulations—they never think about them when buying a car.) Simply put, if I had to choose between a car with high horsepower and a big engine versus one with moderate horsepower, I’d pick the one with moderate horsepower but lots of features, because it’s more practical and beneficial compared to a high-horsepower car that you can never fully utilize... In Vietnam, on city streets, you can only go 30 km/h to 40 km/h. If you drive a powerful car, it’s harder to control. And on suburban roads, it’s only 80 km/h to 90 km/h. How often do you get to drive at the allowed speed? At most, you can go a few kilometers on one road before you hit traffic jams... And when you’re on the expressway, you can only reach a top speed of 120 km/h anyway. How often do you have the chance to experience the expressway? So to sum it up: choosing a car with just enough horsepower but packed with safety and convenience features is better than choosing one with high horsepower, high fuel consumption, but stripped down.

category: Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Other; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Value Perception; Safety & ADAS
parameter: NVH; Engine Noise; Build Quality; Headroom; Driver Visibility; Ride Quality; Resale Value; Steering System
content_english: The Xforce has much better sound insulation. The HRV G version has a pretty stiff ride, but it's still smoother than the Yaris Cross. The HRV feels solid and sturdy, not flimsy like the Xforce. Just closing the door on the Xforce feels loose and rattling like an old car. Closing the door on the HRV feels satisfying. The HRV's fatal weakness is that it's cramped because the roof is too low (a trade-off for the sporty looks). The driver's seat is also low, losing visibility because the mirrors stick out a lot. The Xforce has a wider view. The HRV's middle seat in the second row sticks up; a 1.7m person sitting there will hit the roof – it's only tolerable for kids. In short, if you want spaciousness for the whole family, you have to get the Xforce; if it's for 1-2 people, the HRV gives a more solid driving feel. Depends on your needs. The Xforce will depreciate faster because of lower build quality, and it's quite a bit cheaper (roughly on par with an old Xpander, maybe a bit like a farm vehicle). The upside is the engine is durable. The HRV had a recall for the steering rack issue, and it's being sued abroad too. I don't know if the cars in Vietnam have been fully fixed for VIN 2025 onward (I've seen few steering wheel anomalies). This is based on my personal experience driving both – no salespeople from either brand throw stones. I'm not siding with anyone.

category: Powertrain / Performance; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space
parameter: Engine Power; Cabin Space; Suspension; Suspension
content_english:
Which one to choose?

Dear all, the B-segment crossover segment in Vietnam is really heating up. Here are four standout models, and I'll briefly summarize their pros and cons:

1. Honda HR-V 1.5G, priced at 699 million VND: 119 hp, 145 Nm. Pros: excellent chassis, great handling, spacious, full safety features, and the smartest in the segment. Cons: can be noisy when driving on the highway.

2. Hyundai Creta 1.5L, priced at 640 - 730 million VND: 115 hp, 144 Nm. Pros: best-in-class sound insulation, most attractive and appealing screen in the segment. Cons: engine response not as expected.

3. Kia Seltos 1.4T, priced at 649 - 759 million VND: 138 hp, 242 Nm. Pros: strongest engine in the segment, spacious, and the suspension is solid. Cons: the ride is quite firm.

4. Toyota Corolla Cross 1.8G, priced at 746 million VND: 138 hp, 172 Nm. Pros: smooth and comfortable on mixed roads and in the city, spacious, fuel-efficient on highways. Cons: safety features are just adequate.

In your opinion, which one will dominate the segment in the coming time? And which pair will have the fiercest competition?

Let's share our thoughts!

content_english: I searched on YouTube but no one has compared the VF6 Plus with the HRV G. The reason I'm interested in these two options is because when switching from a motorcycle to a car, what I care about most is safety, so I only consider cars with a full set of ADAS features. In the B-segment, besides the VF6 Plus and HRV G, there's also the Seltos GT-Line but it doesn't have a price yet (expected price is also high, so I've decided to skip it). Comparing the current on-road prices, the HRV G gets a 50% reduction in registration fee from the dealer, so it would be around 73x, and after adding interior upgrades, sensors, and a 360 camera, probably around 77x. The VF6 Plus battery purchase version doesn't have registration fees, and also gets two promotions: a 4% direct discount and a 5% voucher for switching from a Vin gas car to an electric one, so the on-road price is quite similar. For a small family's needs, occasionally going around town, sometimes driving on the highway back to my hometown, and once in a while wanting to go on road trips to the northern mountainous provinces. I hope Mr. Giap will soon make a detailed comparison video of these two cars from every angle.

category: Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Brand Perception
content_english: I rented the X Premium and found it painfully weak, seriously. When starting from a stop, the 4-speed is better than CVT, but when running at 50-60 km/h and wanting to accelerate, the 4-speed gearbox really shows its weakness. The engine revs with the throttle, but since it hasn't reached the downshift threshold, the gear ratio doesn't change, the car feels sluggish and doesn't want to go. You have to stomp the gas much deeper for it to downshift and pull, which is really annoying... Because it only has 4 gears, each gear has to cover a very wide throttle range, so throttle response is terrible... A 1.5L engine paired with a CVT or a 6-speed like the Mazda 3 would be much better. The only good thing is that the interior is good for Grab drivers, so it sells well, but honestly, the 1.5L cars sold in Vietnam now, only the XL7, Ertiga, and Xpander still use the 4AT =]] If Mitsubishi changes the Xpander to CVT, their sales would drop immediately.

category: Safety & ADAS; Styling / Design; Value Perception
parameter: Safety Features; Interior Design; Competitor Comparison
content_english:
For a broken car B and mainly used in the city, I find the Lux is missing two things: cruise control and extra airbags. Many people keep comparing that other cars have ADAS while this one doesn’t, but it’s a matter of choice. It’s like giving two housewives the same limited budget – their grocery lists won’t match. One likes fish so she buys fish, the other likes meat so she buys meat; there’s no way to have a menu with both fish and meat on a tight budget.

Here, I find the 2024 Seltos a bit disappointing in safety features for its price range, but the comfort features are overly generous. The Honda HR-V G is packed with safety, but the interior is bland and the screen isn’t as nice, plus single-zone AC…

If you keep hoping for a car priced at just over 600 with safety features like a Honda and comfort features like a Kia, how could that exist?

That’s my take, feel free to throw stones.

As for the driving feel of ADAS... it's like something sluggish, like a pretty girl. But a pretty girl often has poor inner qualities you can't see. The car's frame is the most expensive and R&D-intensive part. Gasoline engines and transmissions are already saturated – no company is pouring money into developing them anymore. Those options and ADAS are just like makeup: cheap and rinse right off.

The Creta has Bose speakers, so its sound system is better than the HRV's (the HRV's sound is just okay). However, since I'm quite OCD, I chose the HRV, accepting the noise in exchange for a better-built, more solid car with better driving feel and nicer exterior.

The Xforce gives a quiet, smooth driving feel, good sound insulation, and spacious seating. The seats are indeed firmer than other cars, but that's okay for safety; the driver's seat doesn't have power adjustment, and it's not as comfortable as the Cerato I'm currently driving. The Yaris Cross is cramped—the driver's cabin is tight, legs underneath aren't comfortable, so I had to skip it. The HR-V is sturdy, but the seats and rear row design are a bit boring, so I passed on it. I also considered the CX-5, but the price was a bit high and the driving feel wasn't great, so I didn't like it. Back then, the new 2024 Seltos Luxury version was nearly 800 million, so I wasn't into it, but honestly, Kia's interior and exterior are very beautiful. If I could choose again, I might go for the Kia Seltos or go back to a lower ground clearance. High ground clearance makes it too bumpy.^^

content_english: I bought this car at the end of August after test driving many cars from other brands like the City, Yaris Cross, HR-V... No car is perfect, only the one that suits you. The Xforce feels light, quiet, has good sound insulation, and is spacious to sit in. The exterior is indeed a bit thinner than other cars, but if it's safe then it's okay. The driver's seat doesn't have electric adjustment, and it's not as comfortable to sit in as the Cerato I'm currently driving. The Yaris Cross is cramped—the driver's cabin feels tight, legroom isn't comfortable, so I had to skip it. The HR-V is sturdy, but the fabric seats and rear row design are a bit dull, so I passed on it too. I also considered the CX-5, but the price was a bit high and the driving feel wasn't great, so I didn't like it. At that time, the new 2024 Seltos Luxury version was nearly 800, so I didn't go for it, but honestly the interior and exterior of the Kia are very nice. If I could choose again, I might go for the Kia Seltos or switch back to a lower ground clearance. High ground clearance is just too bumpy ^^

content_english: Back then when I was buying a car, I also watched YouTube reviews and was really torn, but in the end I still chose this one. The only thing is with the 1.5L engine, acceleration is a bit weaker compared to turbo cars, and the steering is a bit stiff, but you get what you pay for. Oh well, whatever, I really like it. Especially driving in the city, it's compact and good-looking, easy to squeeze through traffic and park. After nearly 2 years, the car still looks luxurious and classy with no issues. Anyone with a budget around this price range, go ahead and buy it. Oh, and maintenance is cheap too. Let me repeat: you get what you pay for, so don't expect more than that. The important thing is, if you like a certain car brand, just buy that brand.

content_english: Many people quickly jump in to criticize when this video is posted. For a car at this price point, there have to be trade-offs — you get some things and lose others; you can't have everything. The Xforce is quite smart in targeting Vietnamese tastes: investing in eye-catching exterior design, a modern-looking interior, and spacious cabin (since it borrows the Outlander's chassis to compete with other B-segment CUVs); but it has to sacrifice other features: the engine isn't strong while the car is bigger than rivals, so acceleration is sluggish and fuel consumption is higher, steering feel is average, noise insulation is unremarkable, and it cuts out many fancy premium options.

content_english:
Saying "for me, Sensing is a useless feature" is a very blameworthy mindset among all car reviewers in Vietnam.

Okay! You may be a skilled driver, but that doesn't mean everyone on the road drives as well as you! You drive well, but can you dodge a bad driver crashing into you? I can't avoid those weak drivers, so giving them a car with active safety systems is also about protecting myself.

Regarding the recent accident of an electric car in Tuyen Quang, where it swerved to avoid a motorcycle and crashed straight into a house, killing a child—if the car had active safety systems, it wouldn't have plowed into the house like that.

You guys only focus on interior and exterior design, technology, features, and price, while the accident rate for you in that car is very high—don't you care about that?

In South Korea, even the cheapest Hyundai and Kia cars come with an active safety package, while in Vietnam, they strip all of that out, only offering it on cars costing 1 billion VND or more.

I think Honda is the most decent brand for equipping Sensing on all its models, while other brands cut it out because our people don't care about safety, right?

If car reviewers focused on active safety systems, car companies wouldn't look down on our people's lives and safety like this! And when our people buy cars, they wouldn't disregard the safety features the car provides.

category: Comfort & Space; Value Perception; Comfort & Space; Safety & ADAS; Value Perception; Safety & ADAS; Comfort & Space; Value Perception; Styling / Design; Brand Perception
parameter: Cabin Space; Resale Value; NVH; Safety Perception; Build Quality; Safety Feature; Ride Quality; Build Quality; Exterior Design; Country of Origin Image
content_english: I sell online, so I need a big, spacious car to carry goods. I chose the XFC. Since my baby was just born, I don't have a need to travel far. So choosing the XFC for daily commuting to haul goods is fine — I always drive at max speed under 70 km/h, mostly around 30-40 km/h on short trips of about 5-10 km. It makes sense because it's roomy. To be honest, I only bought it for the space. If my kid grows up and can go on long trips, and I don't have to carry goods anymore, I'd buy a Cross in this price range. Because the Cross is an international car, much safer and smoother, with better build quality and smarter design. But with this XFC, when I drive on rough concrete roads, it rattles and creaks — plastic trim around the B-pillar, A-pillar, and the door sill area all make noises. Driving this XFC at high speeds really scares me. I know that cars produced for secondary markets and imported into Vietnam are very poorly built. So later, when my child is bigger, for safety I'll switch to a brand-new Cross. For now, I'll just use the XFC temporarily — after all, this XFC doesn't lose much value, so it's less painful on the wallet.

content_english:
MOON - SUN

Dear sirs,

After spending half a day following the video “Kia Seltos 2024 Luxury review,” I see opinions are split into two camps. Now, I’d really like to hear your thoughts on two completely opposite choices at the same price point:

Option 1: Honda HR-V G trim – prioritizes safety features, for those who value experience, accept just enough comfort features, but must have full safety gear – even if the car is a B-segment, it still needs a complete ADAS package.

Option 2: Kia Seltos Luxury trim – prioritizes comfort, for those who mostly drive in the city, don’t need many automatic safety features that are rarely used, but comfort must stand out in its segment – you sit inside and feel impressed.

If you had to choose one of these two cars, which one would you pick?

I hope you’ll share honestly, and let’s see which side is larger.

It’s very possible that those who BUY the car are not the ones who COMMENT. So let’s follow the sales results from the Vietnam Automobile Manufacturers Association (VAMA) to see if there’s any surprise in the market, and whether our guesses are correct.

Sincerely thank you all for following and supporting Tipcar!

Wishing you and your family a happy weekend!

content_english: I’m a user with a budget of around 700 million VND. Before, I also liked the HRV G – I went to see it in person and took a test drive. The HRV looks quite low and cramped, though the legroom is decent. I’m 1.7m tall and almost hit the ceiling. The front driver’s seat also doesn’t feel as airy as the Creta. The drive feels fairly solid, but the ride is a bit rough. The HRV G has a sporty look that suits someone without a family, because the rear seats are already low, and the middle seat is even higher. After that, I switched to looking at the Creta and instantly liked it. It’s quite big and spacious, with a high, airy ceiling. It has 8 Bose speakers that sound great, good sound insulation, and quite a lot of features. The interior is nicer than the HRV. I bought the car for my family, so I went with the top-spec variant which comes fully loaded. The two-tone paint looks really nice. Now I’ve driven over 1,000 km and still find it the most suitable car in its segment for a family. Driving it, the sound insulation is good, visibility is clear, it’s spacious, and listening to the Bose speakers is very entertaining.

category: Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Styling / Design; Styling / Design; New Tech; Comfort & Space; Value Perception; Value Perception; Comfort & Space; New Tech; Value Perception; Comfort & Space; Styling / Design; Powertrain / Performance; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space
parameter: Cabin Space; Cabin Space; Seat Comfort; Interior Design; Wheel Design; Audio System; NVH; Features / Options; Features / Options; Cabin Space; Audio System; General Impression; Leg Room; Exterior Design; Energy Consumption; Cabin Space; Cabin Space
content_english:
I'm a user with a budget of 700 million VND. Previously, I also liked the HR-V G. I actually went to see and test drive it. The HR-V looks quite low and cramped. The plus is that the footwell is wide, but I'm 1.7m tall and almost hit the ceiling. The front row also isn't as airy as the Creta. When driving, it feels fairly solid, but the ride is quite uncomfortable. The HR-V G has a sporty look, suitable for those without a family, because the rear seats are already low, and the middle seat sticks up even more.

After that, I switched to looking at the Creta and immediately liked it. It's quite big and spacious, with a high, airy ceiling. It has 8 Bose speakers that sound great, good sound insulation, plenty of features, and a nicer interior than the HR-V. I got the car for my family, so I went with the Special Edition, which has quite a full set of features. The machined painted rims look really nice. Now, after driving over 1,000km, I still find it the most suitable in its segment for a family. The car has good sound insulation, an airy view, is spacious, and the Bose speakers are excellent for entertainment.

category: Safety & ADAS; Comfort & Space; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space
parameter: ADAS Features; Ride Quality; Interior Design; Cabin Space; Engine Power; Transmission; Visibility; Rearview Mirror; NVH
content_english: I put down a deposit on the HRV after test-driving almost every B-segment car sold in Vietnam (Seltos, Creta, Xforce, Lynk&Co 06, Yaris Cross, MG ZS, HRV) plus a few small MPVs (BRV, XL7, Xpander). The steering feel, chassis, body, and ADAS on the Honda are truly a different level. Back in the day I always gave Honda flak for being overpriced and I was really into the Xforce, but when I actually test-drove them, handled them in person, and put my money down on a new car, I realized it's no accident that Hondas cost more. Inside, there are lots of leather-wrapped details like the lower dashboard, door panels, and even rubber seals around the door openings, so closing the doors feels very solid. The interior space is also very roomy because Honda optimizes the cabin well; if anything is smaller, it's the trunk because the HRV's rear slopes early for a sporty look. The engine is punchy, there are paddle shifters behind the steering wheel, the A-pillars are slim, and the rearview mirror is big and satisfying to look through. The downside is the tires are quite hard, so on rough roads the tire roar and hollow sound are louder, but not by much compared to the others. Fuel consumption averages about 5L on the highway and 6.5L in the city. Thanks to the channel owner—before putting down my deposit I watched a lot of your videos. Wishing your channel continued growth.

content_english: I paid and confirmed the HRV after test driving nearly all the B-segment SUVs sold in Vietnam (Seltos, Creta, Xforce, Lynk&Co 06, Yaris Cross, MG ZS, HRV) plus a few small MPV models (BRV, XL7, Xpander). The steering feel, chassis, body structure, and ADAS of Honda are truly different. Although I used to only play with Honda's crazy pricing before and was really into the Xforce, when it came time to actually test drive, sit inside firsthand, and put down money for a new car, I realized it's not for nothing that Honda cars cost more. Inside, there are many leather-wrapped details like under the dashboard, door panels, and the door edges have rubber seals for closing, so opening and closing the doors feels very solid. The interior space is also very roomy because Honda optimizes the cabin well; if anything is a bit smaller, it's the trunk, since the HRV's rear slopes early in a sporty style. The engine is punchy, there are paddle shifters behind the steering wheel, the A-pillar is airy, and the rearview mirror is large and very satisfying to use. The downside is the tires are a bit hard, so on rough roads the tire noise inside the wheel wells is louder, but not too much compared to the other cars. Fuel consumption averages around 5L on highways and 6.5L in the city. Thank you, channel owner—before buying, I also watched many of your videos. Wishing the channel continued growth.

category: Powertrain / Performance; Comfort & Space; Styling / Design; Safety & ADAS; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; New Tech; Safety & ADAS; Safety & ADAS; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance
parameter: Steering Feel; Ride Quality; Body Build; ADAS Features; Interior Material Quality; Cabin Space; Trunk Space; Acceleration; Paddle Shifters; Visibility; Rearview Mirror; NVH; Fuel Consumption
content_english: I put down a deposit on the HRV after test driving almost all the B-segment cars available in Vietnam (Seltos, Creta, Xforce, Lynk & Co 06, Yaris Cross, MG ZS, HRV) plus a few compact MPVs (BRV, XL7, Xpander). The steering, chassis, body, and ADAS of the Honda truly feel different. Even though I used to be a Honda fan who paid crazy prices and was really into the Xforce, when it came time to actually test drive, sit in them, and put down money on a new car, I realized there's a reason Hondas cost more. Inside, there are many leather-wrapped details like under the dashboard, door panels, and the door frames even have rubber seals, so opening and closing the doors feels very solid. The interior space is also quite roomy because Honda optimizes the seat area well; the only thing slightly smaller is the trunk, since the HRV's rear slopes earlier for a sporty look. The engine is punchy, there are paddle shifters behind the steering wheel, the A-pillar is slim, and the rearview mirror is large, which is really satisfying. The downside is the tires are a bit hard, so on rough roads the tire noise is louder, but not excessively so compared to the other cars. Fuel consumption averages about 5L on the highway and 6.5L in the city. Thank you to the channel owner; before putting down money, I watched many of your videos. Wishing the channel continued growth.

content_english:
I put down money for the HRV after test driving almost all B-segment SUVs available in Vietnam (Seltos, Creta, Xforce, Lynk&Co 06, Yaris Cross, MG ZS, HRV) plus a few small MPVs like BRV, XL7, and Xpander. The steering feel, chassis, body structure, and ADAS of the Honda are really different. Even though I used to think Honda was overpriced before and I was really into the Xforce, when I actually test drove, sat in, and bought a new car, I realized it's not for nothing that Honda cars cost more. The interior has many leather-wrapped details like under the dashboard, door panels, and even rubber seals around the door closures, so opening and closing the doors feels very solid. The space is also very spacious because Honda optimized the cabin well; the only slightly smaller thing is the transmission area due to the sporty sloping rear of the HRV. The engine is punchy, with paddle shifters behind the steering wheel, the A-pillar is slim, and the rearview mirror is large – very satisfying to look at. The downside is the tires are a bit stiff, so on rough roads the tire noise and boom are a bit louder, but not too much compared to the other cars. Fuel consumption is about 5L on the highway and 6.5L in the city.

Thank you to the channel owner; before buying, I watched many of your videos. Wishing the channel continued growth.

parameter: Wet Road Handling; Engine Noise; Door Closure Quality
content_english: Try driving it on gravel or muddy roads—I haven't ridden on rain-slicked roads yet, but it handles very well, trust me. When you switch to it, you'll feel how solid it is, with clearly better tire grip. I bought the first-generation XF Premium version right when it launched, and it drives quite well. When I test drove the Yaris, the engine noise when accelerating was louder than the XF; the XF's door closure is deeper and has no clicking sounds like the Yaris and Seltos. The air conditioning cools deeply, even in foot mode, and it has a water-cooled compartment for summer. The air filtration mode is very good. The car has minimal odor—even after carrying chickens from the countryside, once you take them out and drive, the smell disappears. This feature might not be available on cars costing a billion. Personally, I choose the XF.

category: Other; Comfort & Space; Styling / Design; Safety & ADAS; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Safety & ADAS; Other; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance
parameter: Steering Feel; Ride Quality; Exterior Design; ADAS Features; Interior Design; Cabin Space; Trunk Space; Engine Power; Transmission; Visibility; Rearview Mirror; NVH; NVH; Energy Consumption
content_english:
I finalized and put down a deposit on the HRV after test driving nearly all the B-segment cars sold in Vietnam (Seltos, Creta, Xforce, Lynk&Co 06, Yaris Cross, MG ZS, HRV) plus a few small MPV models (BRV, XL7, Xpander).  

The steering feel, chassis, body structure, and ADAS of the Honda are truly different. Even though in the past I always criticized Honda for overpricing and really liked the Xforce, when I actually test drove, touched it directly, and put down money for a new car, I realized it's not without reason that Hondas cost more. Inside, there are many leather-wrapped details like under the dashboard, door panels, and the door opening/closing edges have rubber seals, so opening and closing the doors feels very solid. The interior space is also very roomy because Honda optimized the cabin well—the only smaller part is the trunk, since the HRV's rear tapers off early in a sporty style. The engine is punchy, with paddle shifters behind the steering wheel, the A-pillar is thin, and the large rearview mirrors are a joy to use. The downside is the tires are a bit hard; on rough roads the tire noise from the wheel wells is louder, but not excessively more than the other cars. Fuel consumption averages about 5L on the highway and 6.5L in the city.  

Thank you to the channel owner; before putting down money I also watched many of your videos. Wishing the channel continued growth.
